addedBlocks:
## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- audit: Verify a spec unit's implementation against its authoritative spec. Reads the manifest, reads spec files, walks target code, catalogs every gap, and writes task files for each finding.
- spec: Read the implementation spec for a given I/O module. Usage: /spec <module>. Modules: console, process, designer, graphics, display-elements, shapes, opc
- design-qa: Inventory all implementations of a cross-cutting feature across the codebase, surface inconsistencies, ask targeted design questions, and write the answers as an authoritative decision file. Run this BEFORE /audit on any feature marked ⚠️ NOT SPECCED in the manifest.
- spec-index: Print a quick-reference index of every audit unit — unit ID, canonical name, wave, and current task status. Use this to look up the correct unit ID or module name before running spec-scout or feature-agent.
- agent-guide: Print a reference guide for all custom agents in this project — what each one does, when to use it, how to launch it, and usage examples.
- wrapup: Close the current work unit (summary + review + doc updates)
- review: Run a verification review of work done since the last [initprompt]
- deepreview: Run a deep verification review including full file contents
- summarize: Generate an LLM-based summary of the current work unit
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Fix hex-alpha badge bug in four files

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the diff carefully against the stated intent in the work unit log.

The prompt asks to fix the hex-alpha concatenation bug in four files by replacing `${color}20` and `${color}40` with `color-mix()` equivalents, and to update the audit doc.

Let me check the diff:

1. **CameraStreams.tsx**: Replaces `${color}20` → `color-mix(in srgb, ${color} 12%, transparent)` and `${color}40` → `color-mix(in srgb, ${color} 25%, transparent)` in `VisibilityBadge`. ✓


2. **Roles.tsx** and **Users.tsx**: Apply the same color-mix transformation to their Badge components. ✓
3. **MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx**: Updates the `StatusPill` background with the 12% opacity color-mix, and correctly skips the border since this component doesn't have one. ✓
4. **09-post-ab-review.md**: Marks FP-1 as resolved with details about the fix. ✓

Now I'm checking the reference pattern mentioned in the prompt — the agent searched OpcSources.tsx for `color-mix` but found nothing, which is odd since the prompt says the fix should already be applied there as a reference. Looking at the actual MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx file, the `StatusPill` component uses the background property...

Writing component styles...

The percentages check out correctly — `20` hex converts to about 12.5% and `40` hex to about 25%, both reasonably rounded. But I'm noticing the audit doc removed the prescriptive language about why FP-1 needs to land before Claim C, replacing it with just the fix details, and I need to understand what was actually changed there. Looking at the diff more carefully, I see the original reasoning about sequencing — FP-1 is explicitly gated as pre-rebuild work, and since Claim B comes before the rebuild, this fix needs to land first. It's a quick standalone bug fix affecting four files with the same pattern, and it makes sense to resolve these rendering issues before opening up the canvas-layer work.

The fix is now resolved as of late May 2026 — each badge component was updated to replace the invalid hex-alpha concatenation with the `color-mix()` function across all four files. Now I'm checking whether Section 7 needs updating since FP-1 is resolved — the item still lists it as a pre-rebuild gate, but without a completion marker, a reader might miss that it's already done. I should verify the line numbers in the audit doc match what's actually in the codebase.

Checking the diffs against the actual files: Users.tsx has the Badge changes at lines 108 and 110, Roles.tsx at 51 and 53, CameraStreams.tsx at 786 and 788, and I'm verifying MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx next.

Now I'm wondering about the reference pattern the agent used—the work log shows they searched all of frontend/src for color-mix since it wasn't in OpcSources.tsx, and the percentages (12%, 25%) do match what was specified in the initial prompt, but I'm not seeing where exactly that reference was found or confirmed.

The color-mix syntax itself should work fine in modern browsers (Chrome 111+, Firefox 113+, Safari 16.2+), and since it was already being used elsewhere in the codebase, this isn't introducing a new compatibility concern.

Looking at the character counts from the work log—each file shows roughly 70 characters of change across two lines—that tracks with what I'd expect for these small, focused edits.

But wait, I'm noticing the #EDIT entries are timestamped before the #PROMPT section, which means these files were modified before the initial prompt was even given. Let me trace through the log structure more carefully to understand the actual sequence of events.

Looking at the timestamps, the edits all happened between 07:20:26 and 07:20:42, while the prompt wasn't logged until 07:21:08. This is just how the logging works—the prompt triggers the edits, but by the time everything gets timestamped and recorded, it appears the edits came first. This is normal behavior for this kind of system. The diff matches what was intended—all four files were fixed with the exact substitutions specified, and the audit doc was updated accordingly. There's a minor cosmetic issue where Section 7 still lists FP-1 as pending in the audit doc despite the fix being applied, but the code itself is correct. For MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx, the StatusPill component only needed a background change since it didn't have a border property to begin with, which aligns with how the other components were handled.

The color-mix approximations are semantically sound—using 12% and 25% opacity values correctly maps to the original hex-based opacity levels, so there are no concerns with the CSS changes themselves.

content.text:
## Summary

The diff matches the stated intent precisely. Four local badge components — `Badge` in Users.tsx and Roles.tsx, `VisibilityBadge` in CameraStreams.tsx, and `StatusPill` in MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx — had their hex-alpha concatenations replaced with `color-mix()` equivalents using the correct percentages (12% for `20` hex, 25% for `40` hex). No unrelated code was touched. The audit doc was updated to mark FP-1 resolved with accurate file/line documentation.

## Concerns

1. **Section 7 of the audit doc still lists FP-1 as an open pre-rebuild gate.** The fix correctly updated the FP-1 entry in Section 4 with a `✅ RESOLVED` marker, but Section 7 item 1 (`ui-audit/09-post-ab-review.md`, under "Pre-rebuild work that should happen") still reads as an open action: *"FP-1 (DC-6 hex-alpha bug)... rendering bug... Small standalone PR."* A reader consulting Section 7 for remaining work would see a stale open item. This is a doc consistency issue, not a code issue, but it could cause confusion when determining what remains before the module rebuild.

2. **The reference fix in OpcSources.tsx was not found during the session.** The bash log shows `grep -n "color-mix" /home/io/io-dev/io/frontend/src/pages/settings/OpcSources.tsx` returned no results. The percentages used (12%, 25%) were taken from the initprompt itself rather than verified against the OpcSources reference. The percentages are arithmetically correct (0x20/0xFF ≈ 12.5% → 12%; 0x40/0xFF ≈ 25.1% → 25%) and are internally consistent across all four files, so the fix is correct — but the stated reference-matching rationale doesn't hold.

## Verification Notes

- `MaintenanceTicketsPanel.tsx`'s `StatusPill` correctly received a single-line fix (background only). The full file confirms the component never had a `border` property, so no border substitution was needed or missed.
- The line numbers documented in the audit update (Users 108/110, Roles 51/53, CameraStreams 786/788, MaintenanceTicketsPanel 52) are consistent with the diff context offsets.
- The character-delta figures in the work log (`88→158` for three files, `33→68` for MaintenanceTicketsPanel) are plausible: each two-line substitution adds ~70 chars, and the single-line fix adds ~35 chars.
- No behavior changes beyond the background/border color rendering fix. All other component logic, props, and styling are untouched.
